statement.markdown: Given an integer array, determine the minimum number of subarray reversals required to make the entire array stable, meaning that for any indices `i` and `j` where `i < j`, the array remains sorted after reversing the subarray `nums[i..j]. A subarray is considered stable if the subsequence of indices are sorted in ascending order.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input contains an integer `t` denoting the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer `n`, the size of the array. The subsequent line contains `n` space-separated integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: For each test case, output the minimum number of subarray reversals required to make the entire array stable.
statement.notes: The array may contain duplicate elements. The length of the array `n` will be between 1 and 10000.

examples.explanation: One of the possible stable sequences is [5,4,3,2,1]. We need to reverse the subarray [5,4,3,2,1] to get it stable.

hints.text: Think about how you can identify a stable subarray.

editorial.title: Editorial Solution
editorial.intuition: The idea is to maintain a list of subarray reversals.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves trying all possible subarray reversals and checking if the resulting array is stable.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a more efficient approach involving two pointers and a boolean array.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a two-pointer technique to identify subarray reversals and a boolean array to track the stability of subarrays.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that all possible subarray reversals are considered and that the resulting array is stable.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution involves walking through a sample test case and identifying the steps involved in the solution.
commonMistakes: Mistaking a subarray reversal for a stable subarray.
Failing to consider all possible subarray reversals.
